front: What is the interior of a set A in a metric space?
back: The interior of a set A, denoted by Int(A) or A°, is the union of all open sets contained in A. It is the largest open subset of A.

front: Define the closure of a set A in a metric space.
back: The closure of a set A, denoted by Cl(A) or Ā, is the intersection of all closed sets containing A. It is the smallest closed set containing A.

front: What is a limit point (or accumulation point) of a set A?
back: A point x is a limit point of A if every open ball (neighborhood) centered at x contains at least one point of A different from x itself.

front: How is the closure of a set A related to its limit points?
back: The closure of A is equal to the union of A and the set of all limit points of A. That is, Cl(A) = A ∪ {all limit points of A}.

front: What does it mean for a set to be dense in a metric space?
back: A set A is dense in a metric space X if the closure of A is equal to X. That is, every point in X is either in A or is a limit point of A.
